Cyclist Estudo de Caso

Shaini

2025-09-16

Este trabalho consiste na conclusão do curso “Google Data Analytics” da Coursera, no qual realizamos um estudo de caso sobre a empresa fictícia Cyclistic Bike-Share, um programa de compartilhamento de bicicletas em Chicago.

O objetivo principal é analisar o comportamento de usuários casuais e membros anuais, gerando insights que possam orientar estratégias de marketing para aumentar a adesão a assinaturas anuais.

Para esta análise, optou-se por utilizar apenas os dados do primeiro trimestre de 2019 e do primeiro trimestre de 2020, seguindo a recomendação do curso e considerando as limitações de armazenamento de dados e desempenho do RStudio Desktop.

A metodologia adotada inclui:

R – para limpeza, preparação e análise exploratória dos dados; Tableau – para criar visualizações interativas e gráficos detalhados, que facilitam a interpretação dos insights e a apresentação dos resultados.

Esta abordagem permite demonstrar tanto habilidades técnicas em análise de dados quanto capacidade de comunicar resultados de forma visual e estratégica.

library(tidyverse)
## ── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
## ✔ dplyr     1.1.4     ✔ readr     2.1.5
## ✔ forcats   1.0.0     ✔ stringr   1.5.1
## ✔ ggplot2   4.0.0     ✔ tibble    3.3.0
## ✔ lubridate 1.9.4     ✔ tidyr     1.3.1
## ✔ purrr     1.1.0     
## ── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
## ✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
## ✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
## ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors
library(readr)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(scales)
## 
## Attaching package: 'scales'
## 
## The following object is masked from 'package:purrr':
## 
##     discard
## 
## The following object is masked from 'package:readr':
## 
##     col_factor
#Livrarias recomendadas
library(conflicted)
# Set dplyr::filter and dplyr::lag as the default choices
conflict_prefer("filter", "dplyr")
## [conflicted] Will prefer dplyr::filter over any other package.
conflict_prefer("lag", "dplyr")
## [conflicted] Will prefer dplyr::lag over any other package.

#===================== # STEP 1: COLLECT DATA #=====================

#Importando dados de 2019
q1_2019 <- read_csv("C:/Users/shain/Documents/Cyclist Estudo de Caso/Divvy_Trips_2019_Q1.csv")
## Rows: 365069 Columns: 12
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Delimiter: ","
## chr (6): start_time, end_time, from_station_name, to_station_name, usertype,...
## dbl (5): trip_id, bikeid, from_station_id, to_station_id, birthyear
## num (1): tripduration
## 
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
## ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
#Visualizando o meu dataset
head(q1_2019)
## # A tibble: 6 × 12
##    trip_id start_time         end_time       bikeid tripduration from_station_id
##      <dbl> <chr>              <chr>           <dbl>        <dbl>           <dbl>
## 1 21742443 2019-01-01 0:04:37 2019-01-01 0:…   2167          390             199
## 2 21742444 2019-01-01 0:08:13 2019-01-01 0:…   4386          441              44
## 3 21742445 2019-01-01 0:13:23 2019-01-01 0:…   1524          829              15
## 4 21742446 2019-01-01 0:13:45 2019-01-01 0:…    252         1783             123
## 5 21742447 2019-01-01 0:14:52 2019-01-01 0:…   1170          364             173
## 6 21742448 2019-01-01 0:15:33 2019-01-01 0:…   2437          216              98
## # ℹ 6 more variables: from_station_name <chr>, to_station_id <dbl>,
## #   to_station_name <chr>, usertype <chr>, gender <chr>, birthyear <dbl>
#Importando dados de 2020
q1_2020 <- read_csv("C:/Users/shain/Documents/Cyclist Estudo de Caso/Divvy_Trips_2020_Q1.csv")
## Rows: 426887 Columns: 13
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Delimiter: ","
## chr (7): ride_id, rideable_type, started_at, ended_at, start_station_name, e...
## dbl (6): start_station_id, end_station_id, start_lat, start_lng, end_lat, en...
## 
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
## ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
colnames(q1_2020)
##  [1] "ride_id"            "rideable_type"      "started_at"        
##  [4] "ended_at"           "start_station_name" "start_station_id"  
##  [7] "end_station_name"   "end_station_id"     "start_lat"         
## [10] "start_lng"          "end_lat"            "end_lng"           
## [13] "member_casual"
# Verificando o intervalo de datas da coluna started_at
min(q1_2019$start_time)
## [1] "2019-01-01 0:04:37"
max(q1_2019$start_time)
## [1] "2019-03-31 9:59:31"

#==================================================== # STEP 2: WRANGLE DATA AND COMBINE INTO A SINGLE FILE #====================================================

#Renomeando o nome das colunas de q1_2019
(q1_2019 <- rename(q1_2019, 
                   ride_id=trip_id,
                   started_at=start_time,
                   ended_at=end_time,
                   start_station_name=from_station_name,
                   end_station_name=to_station_name,
                   start_station_id=from_station_id,
                   end_station_id=to_station_id,
                   member_casual=usertype))
## # A tibble: 365,069 × 12
##     ride_id started_at         ended_at     bikeid tripduration start_station_id
##       <dbl> <chr>              <chr>         <dbl>        <dbl>            <dbl>

##  2 21742444 2019-01-01 0:08:13 2019-01-01 …   4386          441               44
##  3 21742445 2019-01-01 0:13:23 2019-01-01 …   1524          829               15

# Inspecione o dataframe e procure inconsistências
str(q1_2019)
## spc_tbl_ [365,069 × 12] (S3: spec_tbl_df/tbl_df/tbl/data.frame)

str(q1_2020)
## spc_tbl_ [426,887 × 13] (S3: spec_tbl_df/tbl_df/tbl/data.frame)

##   ..   start_station_name = col_character(),

##   ..   end_station_id = col_double(),
##   ..   start_lat = col_double(),
##   ..   start_lng = col_double(),
##   ..   end_lat = col_double(),
##   ..   end_lng = col_double(),
##   ..   member_casual = col_character()
##   .. )
##  - attr(*, "problems")=<externalptr>

Verificou-se que na dataset de 2019 a unidade da coluna ‘ride_id’ está em números e de 2020 está em caracteres. Preferimos usar o padrão da tabela 2020.

q1_2019 <- mutate(q1_2019, ride_id=as.character(ride_id))
#Juntando os dois dataframe em um só

all_trips <- bind_rows(q1_2019, q1_2020)

Nota sobre as colunas bikeid e rideable_type: As colunas bikeid (2019) e rideable_type (2020) não foram associadas nem usadas na análise porque representam informações diferentes. Além do que, viu-se que não são informações necessárias para essa análise em específico.

  • bikeid identifica cada bicicleta individualmente como número, permitindo rastrear cada unidade em 2019.
  • rideable_type em 2020 apresenta apenas “docked_bike” para todas as viagens, sem distinção entre bicicletas.
#Vendo os valores diferentes da coluna 'rideable_type'
unique(q1_2020$rideable_type)
## [1] "docked_bike"
#Verificando quantas vezes cada valor aparece
table(q1_2020$rideable_type)
## 
## docked_bike 
##      426887

Como não há correspondência direta entre essas colunas nos dois anos, incluí-las poderia gerar confusão ou resultados incorretos. Por isso, foram excluídas do dataset final para focar na análise de comportamento de usuários casuais versus membros anuais. Além disso, exclui-se outras colunas que não possuem correspondencia, nomeadamente latitude (inicial e final), gênero, longitute(inicial e final) e ano de nascimento.

#Removendo colunas que não me servem
all_trips <- all_trips %>% 
  select(-c(bikeid, tripduration,gender, rideable_type,start_lat, start_lng, end_lat, end_lng, birthyear)) 

head(all_trips)
## # A tibble: 6 × 8
##   ride_id started_at ended_at start_station_id start_station_name end_station_id
##   <chr>   <chr>      <chr>               <dbl> <chr>                       <dbl>
## 1 217424… 2019-01-0… 2019-01…              199 Wabash Ave & Gran…             84
## 2 217424… 2019-01-0… 2019-01…               44 State St & Randol…            624
## 3 217424… 2019-01-0… 2019-01…               15 Racine Ave & 18th…            644
## 4 217424… 2019-01-0… 2019-01…              123 California Ave & …            176
## 5 217424… 2019-01-0… 2019-01…              173 Mies van der Rohe…             35
## 6 217424… 2019-01-0… 2019-01…               98 LaSalle St & Wash…             49
## # ℹ 2 more variables: end_station_name <chr>, member_casual <chr>

#====================================================== # STEP 3: CLEAN UP AND ADD DATA TO PREPARE FOR ANALYSIS #====================================================== # Inspect the new table that has been created

colnames(all_trips)  #List of column names
## [1] "ride_id"            "started_at"         "ended_at"          
## [4] "start_station_id"   "start_station_name" "end_station_id"    
## [7] "end_station_name"   "member_casual"
nrow(all_trips)  #How many rows are in data frame?
## [1] 791956
dim(all_trips)  #Dimensions of the data frame?
## [1] 791956      8
head(all_trips)  #See the first 6 rows of data frame.  Also tail(all_trips)
## # A tibble: 6 × 8
##   ride_id started_at ended_at start_station_id start_station_name end_station_id
##   <chr>   <chr>      <chr>               <dbl> <chr>                       <dbl>
## 1 217424… 2019-01-0… 2019-01…              199 Wabash Ave & Gran…             84
## 2 217424… 2019-01-0… 2019-01…               44 State St & Randol…            624
## 3 217424… 2019-01-0… 2019-01…               15 Racine Ave & 18th…            644
## 4 217424… 2019-01-0… 2019-01…              123 California Ave & …            176
## 5 217424… 2019-01-0… 2019-01…              173 Mies van der Rohe…             35
## 6 217424… 2019-01-0… 2019-01…               98 LaSalle St & Wash…             49
## # ℹ 2 more variables: end_station_name <chr>, member_casual <chr>
str(all_trips)  #See list of columns and data types (numeric, character, etc)
## tibble [791,956 × 8] (S3: tbl_df/tbl/data.frame)
##  $ ride_id           : chr [1:791956] "21742443" "21742444" "21742445" "21742446" ...
##  $ started_at        : chr [1:791956] "2019-01-01 0:04:37" "2019-01-01 0:08:13" "2019-01-01 0:13:23" "2019-01-01 0:13:45" ...
##  $ ended_at          : chr [1:791956] "2019-01-01 0:11:07" "2019-01-01 0:15:34" "2019-01-01 0:27:12" "2019-01-01 0:43:28" ...
##  $ start_station_id  : num [1:791956] 199 44 15 123 173 98 98 211 150 268 ...
##  $ start_station_name: chr [1:791956] "Wabash Ave & Grand Ave" "State St & Randolph St" "Racine Ave & 18th St" "California Ave & Milwaukee Ave" ...
##  $ end_station_id    : num [1:791956] 84 624 644 176 35 49 49 142 148 141 ...
##  $ end_station_name  : chr [1:791956] "Milwaukee Ave & Grand Ave" "Dearborn St & Van Buren St (*)" "Western Ave & Fillmore St (*)" "Clark St & Elm St" ...
##  $ member_casual     : chr [1:791956] "Subscriber" "Subscriber" "Subscriber" "Subscriber" ...
summary(all_trips)  #Statistical summary of data. Mainly for numerics
##    ride_id           started_at          ended_at         start_station_id
##  Length:791956      Length:791956      Length:791956      Min.   :  2.0   
##  Class :character   Class :character   Class :character   1st Qu.: 77.0   
##  Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Median :174.0   
##                                                           Mean   :204.4   
##                                                           3rd Qu.:291.0   
##                                                           Max.   :675.0   
##                                                                           
##  start_station_name end_station_id  end_station_name   member_casual     
##  Length:791956      Min.   :  2.0   Length:791956      Length:791956     
##  Class :character   1st Qu.: 77.0   Class :character   Class :character  
##  Mode  :character   Median :174.0   Mode  :character   Mode  :character  
##                     Mean   :204.4                                        
##                     3rd Qu.:291.0                                        
##                     Max.   :675.0                                        
##                     NA's   :1
#Verificando a coluna member_casual
unique(all_trips$member_casual)
## [1] "Subscriber" "Customer"   "member"     "casual"
table(all_trips$member_casual)
## 
##     casual   Customer     member Subscriber 
##      48480      23163     378407     341906
#case_when(
  #condição1 ~ valor1,
  #condição2 ~ valor2,
  #condição3 ~ valor3,
  #TRUE       ~ valor_default  # opcional
#)
#Mudando 'Subscriber' para 'member' e 'Customer' para 'casual' 
all_trips <- all_trips %>% 
  mutate(member_casual = case_when(
    member_casual %in% c("Customer", "casual") ~ "casual",
    member_casual %in% c("Subscriber", "member") ~ "member"
  ) )

Desmembrando data, ano, mês, dia, dia da semana e hora. Lembrando que o formato está “2019-01-01 0:04:37”

#Criando colunas desmembrando data, ano, dia, mes, dia da semana e hora.
#all_trips <- all_trips %>% 
  #mutate(
    #start_date=as.Date(started_at),
    #start_day=day(started_at),
    #start_month=month(started_at),
    #start_year=year(started_at),
    #start_wday= wday(started_at, label=TRUE),
    
    #end_date=as.Date(ended_at),
    #end_day=day(ended_at),
    #end_month=month(ended_at),
    #end_year=year(ended_at),
    #end_wday= wday(ended_at, label=TRUE)
    
    #)
all_trips$date <- as.Date(all_trips$started_at) #The default format is yyyy-mm-dd
all_trips$month <- format(as.Date(all_trips$date), "%m")
all_trips$day <- format(as.Date(all_trips$date), "%d")
all_trips$year <- format(as.Date(all_trips$date), "%Y")
all_trips$day_of_week <- format(as.Date(all_trips$date), "%A")

A primeira vez fiz a conversão de maneira equivocada, o que me deu resultados muito errados no tempo de duração da corrida (mais de 90% do meu trip_duration ficou ZERADO).
Ao analisar o formato de ‘started_at’ e ‘ended_at’, verificou-se o formato de characteres. Ou seja, é necessário mudar isso para POSIXct ou POSIXlt, uma vez que characteres não vai fazer a contagem da diferença de tempo.

all_trips$started_at <- as.POSIXct(all_trips$started_at, format = "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
all_trips$ended_at   <- as.POSIXct(all_trips$ended_at,   format = "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")

all_trips$ride_length <- difftime(all_trips$ended_at, all_trips$started_at, unit="secs")

✅ Resumindo: a s.character → só texto, não faz cálculo. as.Date → só data (perde hora). as.POSIXct → data + hora (certo para calcular duração).

all_trips$ride_length <- as.numeric(all_trips$ride_length, units = "mins")
head(all_trips)
## # A tibble: 6 × 14
##   ride_id  started_at          ended_at            start_station_id
##   <chr>    <dttm>              <dttm>                         <dbl>
## 1 21742443 2019-01-01 00:04:37 2019-01-01 00:11:07              199
## 2 21742444 2019-01-01 00:08:13 2019-01-01 00:15:34               44
## 3 21742445 2019-01-01 00:13:23 2019-01-01 00:27:12               15
## 4 21742446 2019-01-01 00:13:45 2019-01-01 00:43:28              123
## 5 21742447 2019-01-01 00:14:52 2019-01-01 00:20:56              173
## 6 21742448 2019-01-01 00:15:33 2019-01-01 00:19:09               98
## # ℹ 10 more variables: start_station_name <chr>, end_station_id <dbl>,
## #   end_station_name <chr>, member_casual <chr>, date <date>, month <chr>,
## #   day <chr>, year <chr>, day_of_week <chr>, ride_length <dbl>
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(data=all_trips, aes(x=ride_length, day_of_week, color=member_casual)) + geom_point()
## Warning: Removed 36 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_point()`).

Vendo esse gráfico, verificou-se uma grande quantidade de ride_length igual a zero. Vamos chegar essa situação. Alem disso vamos remover as linhas em que o start_station_name equivalem a “HQ QR” pois são viagens testes.

sum(all_trips$ride_length == 0, na.rm = TRUE)
## [1] 93

Verificando os valores que apareceram em ride_length

table(all_trips$member_casual)
## 
## casual member 
##  71643 720313

Dessa forma vemos que existem valor de ride_length negativo e igual a zero. Vamos criar um novo dataset sem esses elementos.

all_trips_v2 <- all_trips[!(all_trips$start_station_name == "HQ QR" | all_trips$ride_length<=0),]

head(all_trips_v2)
## # A tibble: 6 × 14
##   ride_id  started_at          ended_at            start_station_id
##   <chr>    <dttm>              <dttm>                         <dbl>
## 1 21742443 2019-01-01 00:04:37 2019-01-01 00:11:07              199
## 2 21742444 2019-01-01 00:08:13 2019-01-01 00:15:34               44
## 3 21742445 2019-01-01 00:13:23 2019-01-01 00:27:12               15
## 4 21742446 2019-01-01 00:13:45 2019-01-01 00:43:28              123
## 5 21742447 2019-01-01 00:14:52 2019-01-01 00:20:56              173
## 6 21742448 2019-01-01 00:15:33 2019-01-01 00:19:09               98
## # ℹ 10 more variables: start_station_name <chr>, end_station_id <dbl>,
## #   end_station_name <chr>, member_casual <chr>, date <date>, month <chr>,
## #   day <chr>, year <chr>, day_of_week <chr>, ride_length <dbl>
ggplot(data=all_trips_v2, aes(x=ride_length, day_of_week, color=member_casual)) + geom_point() 
## Warning: Removed 36 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_point()`).

table(all_trips_v2$ride_length)
## 
## 0.0166666666666667 0.0333333333333333               0.05 0.0666666666666667 
##                  3                146                233                180

#Brincar novamente com o ride_length pq agora não consigo fazer cálculos como media, mediana, max e min. Isso tem a ver com o formato POSIXct e NUMERIC. Isso será cenas dos próximos capítulos.

#===================================== # STEP 4: CONDUCT DESCRIPTIVE ANALYSIS #=====================================

mean(all_trips_v2$ride_length) #straight average (total ride length / rides)
## [1] NA
median(all_trips_v2$ride_length) #midpoint number in the ascending array of ride lengths
## [1] NA
max(all_trips_v2$ride_length) #longest ride
## [1] NA
min(all_trips_v2$ride_length) #shortest ride
## [1] NA
head(all_trips_v2)
## # A tibble: 6 × 14
##   ride_id  started_at          ended_at            start_station_id
##   <chr>    <dttm>              <dttm>                         <dbl>
## 1 21742443 2019-01-01 00:04:37 2019-01-01 00:11:07              199
## 2 21742444 2019-01-01 00:08:13 2019-01-01 00:15:34               44
## 3 21742445 2019-01-01 00:13:23 2019-01-01 00:27:12               15
## 4 21742446 2019-01-01 00:13:45 2019-01-01 00:43:28              123
## 5 21742447 2019-01-01 00:14:52 2019-01-01 00:20:56              173
## 6 21742448 2019-01-01 00:15:33 2019-01-01 00:19:09               98
## # ℹ 10 more variables: start_station_name <chr>, end_station_id <dbl>,
## #   end_station_name <chr>, member_casual <chr>, date <date>, month <chr>,
## #   day <chr>, year <chr>, day_of_week <chr>, ride_length <dbl>

Apesar de ter tranformado ride_length para as.numeric, ainda não consigo calcular media, mediana, máximos e mínimos, os resultados são ‘NA’. Vou verificar a existencia de NA na coluna “ride_lenght’.

any(is.na(all_trips_v2$ride_length))
## [1] TRUE
sum(is.na(all_trips_v2$ride_length))
## [1] 36

Encontrou-se 24 elementos ‘NA’, sendo assim vou refazer o meu conjunto de dados, para eliminar tbm os NA.

all_trips_v2 <- all_trips[
  !(all_trips$start_station_name == "HQ QR" | 
    all_trips$ride_length < 0 | 
    is.na(all_trips$ride_length)), 
]
summary(all_trips_v2$ride_length)
##      Min.   1st Qu.    Median      Mean   3rd Qu.      Max. 
## 1.700e-02 5.517e+00 8.983e+00 1.982e+01 1.520e+01 1.771e+05

O valor máximo de duração da viagem é 123 dias, hehe. O que não faz muito sentido, vamos eliminar corridas que ultrapassar 1 dia = 86400.

all_trips_v2 <- all_trips_v2[!(all_trips_v2$ride_length >84600),]

13

#aggregate(variável ~ grupo, FUN = função)

Calcula a média/mediana/máx./min do ride_length para cada grupo de usuários (“member” e “casual”).

aggregate(all_trips_v2$ride_length ~ all_trips_v2$member_casual, FUN = mean)
##   all_trips_v2$member_casual all_trips_v2$ride_length
## 1                     casual                 78.11939
## 2                     member                 12.85113
aggregate(all_trips_v2$ride_length ~ all_trips_v2$member_casual, FUN = median)
##   all_trips_v2$member_casual all_trips_v2$ride_length
## 1                     casual                23.216667
## 2                     member                 8.466667
aggregate(all_trips_v2$ride_length ~ all_trips_v2$member_casual, FUN = max)
##   all_trips_v2$member_casual all_trips_v2$ride_length
## 1                     casual                 80991.13
## 2                     member                 79157.52
aggregate(all_trips_v2$ride_length ~ all_trips_v2$member_casual, FUN = min)
##   all_trips_v2$member_casual all_trips_v2$ride_length
## 1                     casual               0.03333333
## 2                     member               0.01666667
mean_ride <- aggregate(all_trips_v2$ride_length ~ all_trips_v2$member_casual, FUN = mean)
median_ride <- aggregate(all_trips_v2$ride_length ~ all_trips_v2$member_casual, FUN = median)
max_ride <- aggregate(all_trips_v2$ride_length ~ all_trips_v2$member_casual, FUN = max)
min_ride <- aggregate(all_trips_v2$ride_length ~ all_trips_v2$member_casual, FUN = min)

Vendo a média por dia da semana para cada tipo de membro

aggregate(all_trips_v2$ride_length~all_trips_v2$member_casual + all_trips_v2$day_of_week, FUN=mean)
##    all_trips_v2$member_casual all_trips_v2$day_of_week all_trips_v2$ride_length
## 1                      casual                   Friday                 86.83656
## 2                      member                   Friday                 13.27733
## 3                      casual                   Monday                 79.19011
## 4                      member                   Monday                 12.00336
## 5                      casual                 Saturday                 82.45941
## 6                      member                 Saturday                 16.22849
## 7                      casual                   Sunday                 67.02062
## 8                      member                   Sunday                 16.21671
## 9                      casual                 Thursday                 94.15306
## 10                     member                 Thursday                 11.78634
## 11                     casual                  Tuesday                 76.01365
## 12                     member                  Tuesday                 12.02969
## 13                     casual                Wednesday                 74.66507
## 14                     member                Wednesday                 11.86541
# Analyze ridership data by type and weekday
all_trips_v2 %>% 
  mutate(weekday = wday(started_at, label = TRUE)) %>%  # cria coluna com o dia da semana
  group_by(member_casual, weekday) %>%  # agrupa por tipo de usuário e dia da semana
  summarise(
    number_of_rides = n(),  # conta o número de viagens
    average_duration = mean(ride_length, na.rm = TRUE)  # calcula a média de duração sem NA
  ) %>%     
  arrange(member_casual, weekday)  # ordena
## `summarise()` has grouped output by 'member_casual'. You can override using the
## `.groups` argument.
## # A tibble: 14 × 4
## # Groups:   member_casual [2]
##    member_casual weekday number_of_rides average_duration
##    <chr>         <ord>             <int>            <dbl>
##  1 casual        Sun               18642             67.0
##  2 casual        Mon                5591             79.2
##  3 casual        Tue                7311             76.0
##  4 casual        Wed                7690             74.7
##  5 casual        Thu                7145             94.2
##  6 casual        Fri                8012             86.8
##  7 casual        Sat               13473             82.5
##  8 member        Sun               60168             16.2
##  9 member        Mon              110428             12.0
## 10 member        Tue              127973             12.0
## 11 member        Wed              121902             11.9
## 12 member        Thu              125228             11.8
## 13 member        Fri              115168             13.3
## 14 member        Sat               59413             16.2

Mesma coisa do código anterior só que acrescenta um gráfico:

all_trips_v2 %>% 
  mutate(weekday = wday(started_at, label = TRUE)) %>% 
  group_by(member_casual, weekday) %>% 
  summarise(number_of_rides = n()
            ,average_duration = mean(ride_length)) %>% 
  arrange(member_casual, weekday)  %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = weekday, y = number_of_rides, fill = member_casual)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge")
## `summarise()` has grouped output by 'member_casual'. You can override using the
## `.groups` argument.

all_trips_v2 %>% 
  mutate(weekday = wday(started_at, label = TRUE)) %>% 
  group_by(member_casual, weekday) %>% 
  summarise(number_of_rides = n()
            ,average_duration = mean(ride_length)) %>% 
  arrange(member_casual, weekday)  %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = weekday, y = average_duration, fill = member_casual)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge")
## `summarise()` has grouped output by 'member_casual'. You can override using the
## `.groups` argument.

Importando para arquivo.csv

counts <- aggregate(all_trips_v2$ride_length ~ all_trips_v2$member_casual + all_trips_v2$day_of_week, FUN = mean)

write.csv(counts, file = "C:/Users/shain/Documents/Cyclist Estudo de Caso/avg_ride_length.csv", row.names = FALSE)
library(dplyr)

counts <- all_trips_v2 %>%
  group_by(member_casual, day_of_week) %>%
  summarise(
    avg_duration = mean(ride_length),
    total_rides = n()
  )
## `summarise()` has grouped output by 'member_casual'. You can override using the
## `.groups` argument.
write.csv(counts, file = "C:/Users/shain/Documents/Cyclist Estudo de Caso/avg_ride_length_v2.csv", row.names = FALSE)
